title: Ink on Cotton
stainChemistry: Dye stains (ink, markers, food coloring, dye transfer) are pigment molecules suspended in binders or vehicles. Fresh dyes are reversible; aging allows cross-linking with the fiber. VDS (volatile dry solvent) dissolves the pigment and binder, lifting the stain from the fiber. Amyl acetate is stronger but riskier on delicate fabrics. H₂O₂ 6% provides an oxidizing alternative, especially for natural food dyes. Sodium hydrosulfite reduces the dye's chromophores, eliminating color without bleaching the base fiber.
whyThisWorks: Ink is a dye in an oil-based carrier designed to permanently mark surfaces. Cotton absorbs quickly and holds stains deep in the weave. Alcohol or solvent dissolves the carrier holding the dye in place, letting you lift it away before it bonds permanently.
defaultAssumption: Treat as unknown age / possibly dried.

steps: 1. Apply VDS to dissolve the dye pigment and its binder. VDS is gentle but effective — it lifts color molecules from the fiber. Blot with clean cloth, replacing often as it picks up dye. Work from outside in.

2. If VDS plateaus, switch to amyl acetate for stronger solvent action. This targets dye molecules that have bonded more deeply to the fiber. Test on a hidden area first. Never use on acetate fabrics.
3. Flush with NSD to remove all solvent residue and any remaining pigment particles. Rinse thoroughly with cool water.
professionalProtocol.temperature: cold — water causes dye to absorb deeper; treat dry-side first before any wet treatment
warnings: Using water first — water causes dye to absorb deeper into the fiber
Rubbing or agitating the stain — spreads the dye throughout the fabric
Not testing on hidden areas first — some dyes transfer and re-stain adjacent fibers
Oversaturating with solvent — creates a dye-solvent slurry harder to remove than the original stain

homeSolutions.agent: Patch test + identify the ink type
homeSolutions.instruction: Before you treat, check: is this ballpoint, gel pen, permanent marker, highlighter, or printer ink? Ballpoint responds best to alcohol; gel pen and permanent marker often need a dry-cleaning solvent (Amodex or dry-cleaning spotter). Printer ink is water-based and often lifts with dish soap alone. On a hidden seam, dab isopropyl alcohol — if no dye transfers to your cloth from the cotton itself, you're safe to proceed.

homeSolutions.agent: The dye rule: no water first
homeSolutions.instruction: Water drives ink pigment deeper into cotton fiber and makes a small mark into a bigger faded one. Treat dry-side first — alcohol or solvent, never water first. Also: do NOT put the garment in the dryer until the stain is fully out. Heat polymerizes the dye into the cotton permanently.

homeSolutions.agent: Place an absorbent pad UNDER the stain
homeSolutions.instruction: Lay the garment stain-side DOWN on a stack of clean white paper towels or a folded white cloth. As you apply solvent from above, the dissolved ink will transfer DOWN into the pad instead of spreading sideways on the fabric you're saving. This one step does more for ink removal than most people's entire DIY attempt. Replace the pad every time you see it pick up dye.

Dip a clean white cloth or Q-tip in 91% isopropyl alcohol (drugstore). Press it directly onto the stain from above — dab, lift, dab again. Work from outside edges toward the center so you pull dye inward, not outward. Replace the pad underneath every minute or two as it picks up ink. Keep going until no more color transfers. Fresh ballpoint usually lifts completely here.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: Never use alcohol on silk, rayon, acetate, or wool. Cotton tolerates it well. Do not saturate the fabric — pressed dabs beat soaking.

homeSolutions.agent: Dawn dish soap — emulsify what alcohol left behind
homeSolutions.instruction: Once the dye has stopped transferring to the pad, put a small drop of clear Dawn directly on the stain. Work it in with a fingertip for 30 seconds, then let it sit 5 minutes. Dawn emulsifies the oil carrier that most pens use to bind dye to surfaces — alcohol dissolves the dye, Dawn lifts the carrier. Blot with a clean damp cloth.

If a shadow remains, apply Amodex Ink & Stain Remover (drugstore/Amazon, made specifically for ink) per label and dwell 10–15 minutes. Or try a hair spray with alcohol as the active solvent — old trick, still works on ballpoint. For permanent marker: dry-cleaning solvent (Carbona Stain Devil #3) is better than anything water-based. Blot with a clean damp cloth.

Launder the garment on cold, with your normal detergent. Do NOT use hot water and do NOT tumble-dry. Hang or lay flat, inspect in daylight after drying. If any shadow remains, repeat steps 4–6 before drying on heat. Only put in the dryer once the stain is completely invisible in natural light.

On white cotton ONLY, if a pale shadow persists: dab with 3% hydrogen peroxide, let sit 5 minutes, rinse cold. Peroxide oxidizes residual dye color. Do not use on colored cotton — it will bleach the dye. Never mix with bleach or ammonia.

Permanent marker, set-in gel pen, or printer-ink that's been through the dryer usually needs professional solvent chemistry (amyl acetate, VDS) that isn't safe at home. Take it in and tell them: "[Ballpoint / gel / permanent marker / printer] ink on cotton, treated at home with isopropyl alcohol + Dawn + [Amodex / nothing]. No heat applied. Please use dry-side solvent first, then wet-side." That gives them the full treatment history.

steps: 1. Dab the stain with a cloth dipped in rubbing alcohol (isopropyl alcohol 91%, also called IPA).
2. Work from the outside edge toward the center. Swap to a clean part of the cloth often.
3. Keep dabbing until no more color transfers to the cloth.
5. Air dry — don't use a dryer until the stain is gone.
diyProtocol.whenToStop: For permanent markers or set-in dyes, a professional cleaner has access to specialized solvents (like amyl acetate) that work better than household rubbing alcohol. Don't keep treating — you risk damaging the fabric.
warnings: Always blot — never rub with pressure. Rubbing the stain pushes it deeper into the fabric.
Use a white cloth to avoid transferring dye from a colored cloth.
Rubbing alcohol (isopropyl alcohol / IPA) works on most dye stains because it dissolves the carrier that holds the pigment.

neverDo: Apply water first — causes dye to absorb deeper into the fiber
Rub or agitate the stain — spreads the dye throughout the fabric
Use heat before the dye is removed — polymerizes dye into the fiber
fiberSensitivities: Cotton tolerates solvents well. Acetate fibers cannot tolerate amyl acetate or solvents — VDS only. For colored cotton, test H₂O₂ on a hidden seam before use.

escalation.when: VDS plateau reached and dye has likely covalently bonded to the fiber
escalation.whatToTell: Ink stain on cotton, dye-based. Treated at home with rubbing alcohol (isopropyl alcohol, IPA). Please use amyl acetate or professional solvent protocol.

contextQuestions: Is this ballpoint, gel pen, marker, or printer ink?
Is the stain fresh or has it been sitting?
Has the garment been through the dryer?
Is the cotton white or colored?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Permanent marker and gel pen use different chemical formulations than ballpoint — may require stronger solvent. Dryer exposure makes removal very difficult. Fresh stains respond significantly better. On colored cotton, test peroxide first.
